The sins people commit have little impact on their minds, yet every sin is recorded in God’s book; they are all so deeply etched into the heart that they will all be remembered by conscience. What is etched into the heart becomes evident in a person's life; people's actions reveal the desires and intentions of their hearts. How much we need to humble ourselves before God, who sees us as so vile! How we should depend on his mercy and grace, asking God to search and examine us; not to allow us to be deceived by our own hearts, but to create in us a pure and holy nature through his Spirit!

Whoever puts confidence in people will be like a heath in the desert, a bare tree, a pathetic shrub, the product of barren ground, useless and worthless. Those who trust in their own righteousness and strength, and think they can do without Christ, rely on human power, and their souls cannot prosper in grace or comfort. Whoever makes God his hope will flourish like a tree always green, whose leaf does not wither. He will be stable in peace and satisfaction of mind; he will not be anxious during times of drought. Whoever makes God his hope has enough in him to satisfy all needs for creature comforts. He will continue to produce fruit in holiness and good works. The heart, the conscience of people, in their corrupt and fallen state, is deceitful above all things. It calls evil good, and good evil; and declares peace to those to whom it does not belong. Herein the heart is desperately wicked; it is deadly, it is desperate. The situation is truly bad if the conscience, which should correct the errors of other abilities, leads in deception. We cannot know our own hearts, nor what they will do during a time of temptation. Who can understand his errors? Much less can we know the hearts of others or depend on them. Whoever believes God’s testimony in this matter and learns to watch his own heart will find this is an accurate, though sad, picture, and learn many lessons to guide his conduct. But much in our own hearts and in the hearts of others will remain unknown. Yet whatever wickedness there is in the heart, God sees it. People may be deceived, but God cannot be fooled. Whoever gains riches dishonestly, even if he makes them his hope, will never experience joy from them. This shows how distressing it is for a worldly person at death that he must leave his riches behind; but though wealth will not follow to another world, guilt will, and everlasting torment. The rich man takes pains to acquire an estate, and sits brooding over it, but never finds satisfaction in it; through sinful ways it comes to nothing. Let us be wise during our time; whatever we gain, let us get it honestly; and whatever we have, use it generously, so that we may be wise for eternity.

The prophet acknowledges God’s favor in establishing religion. There is fullness of comfort in God, an overflowing, ever-flowing fullness, like a fountain. It is always fresh and clear, like spring water, while the pleasures of sin are like stagnant puddles. He prays to God for healing, saving mercy. He appeals to God concerning his faithful fulfillment of the office to which he was called. He humbly asks that God would approve and protect him in the work to which he had clearly called him. Whatever wounds or diseases we find in our hearts and consciences, let us turn to the Lord to heal us, to save us, so that our souls may praise his name. His hands can bind up a troubled conscience and heal a broken heart; he can cure the worst illnesses of our nature.

The prophet was to present to the rulers and people of Judah the command to keep holy the Sabbath day. Let them strictly observe the fourth commandment. If they obeyed this word, their prosperity would be restored. It is a day of rest, and must not be made a day of labor unless in cases of necessity. Be careful, watch against profaning the Sabbath. Do not let your soul be burdened with the cares of this world on Sabbath days. The flow of religion runs deep or shallow depending on whether the banks of the Sabbath are maintained or neglected. The degree of strictness with which this ordinance is observed, or the neglect shown toward it, is a good test to determine the state of spiritual religion in any land. Let all, by their own example and by attending to their families, strive to stop this evil, so that national prosperity may be preserved, and above all, so that souls may be saved.
